75 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:蓮台寺 (倉敷市) up to rev 104236529
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 蓮台寺 |画像 = [[File:Rendaiji 01.jpg|270px]]
総本殿 |所在地 = 岡山県倉敷市児島由加2855番地 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 30|緯度秒 = 20.25 |経度度 = 133|経度分 = 51|経度秒 = 2.5 |山号 = 由加山 |宗派 = [[真言宗御室派]] |寺格 = 別格本山 |本尊 = [[十一面観音菩薩]]
[[瑜伽大権現]] |創建年 = 伝・[[738年]]（[[天平]]10年） |開基 = 伝・[[行基]] |正式名 = 由加山 瑜伽大権現 蓮台寺 |別称 = |札所等 = 中国三十三観音6番
百八観音8番 |文化財 = 客殿・多宝塔・菊慈童図屏風・梵鐘（県重要文化財）
銅剣（市重要文化財） |地図2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=蓮台寺|float=center}} }} '''蓮台寺'''（れんだいじ）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]][[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]]にある[[真言宗御室派]]の寺で別格本山。山号は[[由加山]]。本尊は[[十一面観音菩薩]]、[[瑜伽大権現]]。[[中国三十三観音霊場]]第6番札所、百八観音霊場第八番札所。 == 歴史 == [[天平]]10年（[[738年]]）[[行基]]が本尊である十一面観音菩薩と[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[薬師如来]]の[[垂迹]]である[[瑜伽大権現]]を祀って創建したのが始まりと伝えられる[http://www.yugasan.jp/engi.html 由加山蓮台寺 – 縁起]。[[平安時代]]末兵火にあって衰退したが、[[室町時代]]に入り増吽[[僧正]]によって中興された。[[江戸時代]]には[[備前国]][[岡山藩]]の保護を受けた。古くから瑜伽大権現は信仰を集めてきたが、明治初年の[[神仏分離]]に伴い蓮台寺境内から由加神社（後の[[由加神社本宮]]）がつくられ、分けられた。 [[第二次世界大戦]]後の[[国家神道]]解体により、形式的には分離したままながら、僧職が本殿で祈祷を行う等事実上明治以前の形態に復し、蓮台寺により両者が一体的に運営されていた。このため由加神社としての[[宗教法人]]資格は一応保持し、神職も配置していたが、事実上休眠状態に近かった。 [[1997年]]（[[平成]]9年）[[3月3日]]、由加神社が一部の建物を占拠し、宗教法人としての独自活動を開始した。これ以降蓮台寺と由加神社は対立関係となり、蓮台寺が建物の明け渡しを求めるなど裁判沙汰にもなるが、由加神社側が独自の宗教活動を開始した経緯は当時の兄（蓮台寺住職）と弟（由加神社宮司）の間の対立が原因とされている。 由加山の[[瑜伽大権現]]と[[讃岐国]]にあった[[金毘羅大権現]]（現[[金刀比羅宮]]）を参る'''[[両参り]]'''と呼ばれる慣習が、江戸時代にはあったという。 == 概要 == '''厄除けの権現さま'''とて信仰を集めている。2007年（[[平成]]19年）総高7メートル59センチメートルの日本一の木造仏像の[[不動明王]]が完成した[http://www.yugasan.jp/19nen.html 由加山蓮台寺 - 由加山厄よけ不動]。 由加山参道は[[門前町]]が形成されており、[[あんころ餅]]が名物となっている。 ==文化財== *岡山県指定重要文化財 **客殿 **多宝塔 **菊慈童図屏風[http://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/5428.htm 菊慈童図屏風] - 倉敷市 桃山から江戸初期 車輪のような描き方の松葉に様式化が見られるものの、勢いのある松や岩肌の力強い[[皴法]]を持ち、[[海北友松]]や[[狩野山楽]]らの作風に通じる。 **梵鐘 *倉敷市指定重要文化財 **銅剣 ==所在地== *岡山県倉敷市児島由加2855番地 == アクセス == *[[瀬戸中央自動車道]][[水島インターチェンジ|水島IC]]より[[岡山県道21号岡山児島線]]を岡山方面へ向かい「郷内」交差点にて[[岡山県道62号玉野福田線]]へ東折。そのまま同県道上を道なりに直進。車で約15分。 *[[瀬戸大橋線|JR瀬戸大橋線]]・[[児島駅]]よりタクシー約15分。 *瀬戸大橋線・[[上の町駅]]より徒歩で約4.5キロメートル。 *かつては[[児島駅]]より下電バス由加山行きが運行されていたが2020年9月30日に廃止されたため、路線バスでのアクセスは不可能。王子ヶ岳線「由加登山口」バス停からは約5キロメートルあるため注意。 == 隣の札所 == ;[[中国三十三観音霊場]] :5 [[法界院]] -- '''6 蓮台寺''' -- 7 [[円通寺 (倉敷市)|円通寺]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991年 96-97ページ *現地説明板 == 関連項目 == * [[由加神社本宮]] * [[熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] * [[柴田義董]] - 50面の蓮台寺障壁画を描いている。 ==外部リンク== {{commons|Category:Rendaiji}} *[https://yugasan.jp/ 由加山蓮台寺] *{{Facebook|yugasanrendaiji|由加山　蓮台寺}} *{{Twitter|rendai_ji|由加山蓮台寺}} *[https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ 倉敷市文化財保護課] {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:れんたいし くらしき}} [[Category:岡山県の寺]] [[Category:真言宗御室派の寺院]] [[Category:瑜伽信仰]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:倉敷市の建築物]] [[Category:倉敷市の歴史]] [[Category:中国三十三観音霊場]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:岡山県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:行基|伝]] {{buddhism-stub}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)]] to [[Yugasan Rendaiji]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 蓮台寺 |画像 = [[File:Rendaiji 01.jpg|270px]]
総本殿 |所在地 = 岡山県倉敷市児島由加2855番地 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 30|緯度秒 = 20.25 |経度度 = 133|経度分 = 51|経度秒 = 2.5 |山号 = 由加山 |宗派 = [[真言宗御室派]] |寺格 = 別格本山 |本尊 = [[十一面観音菩薩]]
[[瑜伽大権現]] |創建年 = 伝・[[738年]]（[[天平]]10年） |開基 = 伝・[[行基]] |正式名 = 由加山 瑜伽大権現 蓮台寺 |別称 = |札所等 = 中国三十三観音6番
百八観音8番 |文化財 = 客殿・多宝塔・菊慈童図屏風・梵鐘（県重要文化財）
銅剣（市重要文化財） |地図2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=蓮台寺|float=center}} }} '''蓮台寺'''（れんだいじ）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]][[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]]にある[[真言宗御室派]]の寺で別格本山。山号は[[由加山]]。本尊は[[十一面観音菩薩]]、[[瑜伽大権現]]。[[中国三十三観音霊場]]第6番札所、百八観音霊場第八番札所。 == 歴史 == [[天平]]10年（[[738年]]）[[行基]]が本尊である十一面観音菩薩と[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[薬師如来]]の[[垂迹]]である[[瑜伽大権現]]を祀って創建したのが始まりと伝えられる[http://www.yugasan.jp/engi.html 由加山蓮台寺 – 縁起]。[[平安時代]]末兵火にあって衰退したが、[[室町時代]]に入り増吽[[僧正]]によって中興された。[[江戸時代]]には[[備前国]][[岡山藩]]の保護を受けた。古くから瑜伽大権現は信仰を集めてきたが、明治初年の[[神仏分離]]に伴い蓮台寺境内から由加神社（後の[[由加神社本宮]]）がつくられ、分けられた。 [[第二次世界大戦]]後の[[国家神道]]解体により、形式的には分離したままながら、僧職が本殿で祈祷を行う等事実上明治以前の形態に復し、蓮台寺により両者が一体的に運営されていた。このため由加神社としての[[宗教法人]]資格は一応保持し、神職も配置していたが、事実上休眠状態に近かった。 [[1997年]]（[[平成]]9年）[[3月3日]]、由加神社が一部の建物を占拠し、宗教法人としての独自活動を開始した。これ以降蓮台寺と由加神社は対立関係となり、蓮台寺が建物の明け渡しを求めるなど裁判沙汰にもなるが、由加神社側が独自の宗教活動を開始した経緯は当時の兄（蓮台寺住職）と弟（由加神社宮司）の間の対立が原因とされている。 由加山の[[瑜伽大権現]]と[[讃岐国]]にあった[[金毘羅大権現]]（現[[金刀比羅宮]]）を参る'''[[両参り]]'''と呼ばれる慣習が、江戸時代にはあったという。 == 概要 == '''厄除けの権現さま'''とて信仰を集めている。2007年（[[平成]]19年）総高7メートル59センチメートルの日本一の木造仏像の[[不動明王]]が完成した[http://www.yugasan.jp/19nen.html 由加山蓮台寺 - 由加山厄よけ不動]。 由加山参道は[[門前町]]が形成されており、[[あんころ餅]]が名物となっている。 ==文化財== *岡山県指定重要文化財 **客殿 **多宝塔 **菊慈童図屏風[http://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/5428.htm 菊慈童図屏風] - 倉敷市 桃山から江戸初期 車輪のような描き方の松葉に様式化が見られるものの、勢いのある松や岩肌の力強い[[皴法]]を持ち、[[海北友松]]や[[狩野山楽]]らの作風に通じる。 **梵鐘 *倉敷市指定重要文化財 **銅剣 ==所在地== *岡山県倉敷市児島由加2855番地 == アクセス == *[[瀬戸中央自動車道]][[水島インターチェンジ|水島IC]]より[[岡山県道21号岡山児島線]]を岡山方面へ向かい「郷内」交差点にて[[岡山県道62号玉野福田線]]へ東折。そのまま同県道上を道なりに直進。車で約15分。 *[[瀬戸大橋線|JR瀬戸大橋線]]・[[児島駅]]よりタクシー約15分。 *瀬戸大橋線・[[上の町駅]]より徒歩で約4.5キロメートル。 *かつては[[児島駅]]より下電バス由加山行きが運行されていたが2020年9月30日に廃止されたため、路線バスでのアクセスは不可能。王子ヶ岳線「由加登山口」バス停からは約5キロメートルあるため注意。 == 隣の札所 == ;[[中国三十三観音霊場]] :5 [[法界院]] -- '''6 蓮台寺''' -- 7 [[円通寺 (倉敷市)|円通寺]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991年 96-97ページ *現地説明板 == 関連項目 == * [[由加神社本宮]] * [[熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] * [[柴田義董]] - 50面の蓮台寺障壁画を描いている。 ==外部リンク== {{commons|Category:Rendaiji}} *[https://yugasan.jp/ 由加山蓮台寺] *{{Facebook|yugasanrendaiji|由加山　蓮台寺}} *{{Twitter|rendai_ji|由加山蓮台寺}} *[https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ 倉敷市文化財保護課] {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:れんたいし くらしき}} [[Category:岡山県の寺]] [[Category:真言宗御室派の寺院]] [[Category:瑜伽信仰]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:倉敷市の建築物]] [[Category:倉敷市の歴史]] [[Category:中国三十三観音霊場]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:岡山県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:行基|伝]] {{buddhism-stub}}

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu|ja|十一面観音菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of {{ill|Daibō Era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in the Kojima area of {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (738), the monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the {{ill|Okayama Han|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu|ja|十一面観音菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of {{ill|Daibō Era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in the Kojima area of {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (738), the monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the {{ill|Okayama Han|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

[[sn:Yugasan Rendaiji]] [[ja:蓮台寺 (倉敷市)]] [[ja:瑜伽山蓮台寺]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu|ja|十一面観音菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of {{ill|Daibō Era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in the Kojima area of {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (738), the monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the {{ill|Okayama Han|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

[[sn:Yugasan Rendaiji]] [[ja:蓮台寺 (倉敷市)]] [[ja:瑜伽山蓮台寺]]

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu|ja|十一面観音菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of {{ill|Daibō Era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in the Kojima area of {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (738), the monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the {{ill|Okayama Han|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki City|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Era|qq=}} (738), the monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the {{ill|Okayama Han|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Han|qq=}} of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintōhira-gū|qq=}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setchu Chūō Expressway|qq=}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintōhira-gū|qq=}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setchu Chūō Expressway|qq=}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki City|qq=}}, Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu|ja|十一面観音|1=Ekādaśamukha|2=ja|3=十一面観音|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=}}{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of {{ill|Daibō Era|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibō Era|qq=}} (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of {{ill|Kurashiki City|ja|倉敷市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki City|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Era|qq=}} (738), the monk {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the {{ill|Okayama Han|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Han|qq=}} of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at {{ill|Kintōhira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintōhira-gū|qq=}}) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the {{ill|Setchu Chūō Expressway|ja|瀬戸中央自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setchu Chūō Expressway|qq=}} at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from {{ill|Kojima Station|ja|児島駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto Ohashi Line|ja|瀬戸大橋線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|中国三十三観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
