{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

(Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division] {{Normdaten}}

The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.

The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

(Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.

== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

[[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金毘羅権現}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水島インターチェンジ}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道21号岡山児島線}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道62号玉野福田線}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金毘羅権現}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水島インターチェンジ}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道21号岡山児島線}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道62号玉野福田線}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金毘羅権現}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水島インターチェンジ}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道21号岡山児島線}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道62号玉野福田線}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==

{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]

{{infobox religious building | name = Yugasan Rendaiji | native_name = {{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:{{ill|Rendaiji Main Hall|ja|蓮台寺 01|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji Main Hall|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.jpg|270px]] | image description = Main Hall (Sōhonden) | address = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], Kojima Yuka 2855 | latitude = 34 | latitude minutes = 30 | latitude seconds = 20.25 | longitude = 133 | longitude minutes = 51 | longitude seconds = 2.5 | mountain_name = {{ill|Yuka-yama|ja|由加山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka-yama|qq=}} | sect = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism, Mimuro-ha|qq=}} | temple_Ranking = Classified as an “Extraordinary Head Temple” | main_image = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Kannon]]{{ill|Yogācāra Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yogācāra Daigongen|qq=}} | founded = Tradition: Year 3 of [[:en:Taiho|Daibō Era]] (703) | founder = | official_name = Yuka-yama Yogācāra Daigongen Rendaiji | alternate_names = | pilgrimage = [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] No. 6{{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 8 | cultural_properties = Guest Hall, Tahō Tower, Kikujidō Illustrated Folding Screen, and the Bell (Designated as a Prefectural Important Cultural Property)Copper Sword (Designated as a City Important Cultural Property) | map2 = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=11|marker=religious-buddhist|frame-width=240|frame-latitude=34.5|frame-longitude=133.88}}{{Location map|Japan Okayama|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=12|label=Rendaiji|float=center}} }} '''Yugasan Rendaiji''' ({{ill|Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji|qq=}}) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Mimuro Sect|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Mimuro Sect|qq=}} located in the Kojima area of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki City]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is classified as an Extraordinary Head Temple. Its mountain name is Yuka-yama (由加山), and its principal images are the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu and Yogācāra Daigongen. It is pilgrimage site No. 6 on the [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] and No. 8 on the {{ill|Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|ja|百八観音霊場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachi Kannon Pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History == Tradition holds that in the 10th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] (738), the monk [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] enshrined the Eleven-faced Kannon Bosatsu (the principal image) along with Yogācāra Daigongen (a manifestation of the trace of Amida and Yakushi) and thus founded the temple. Although the temple declined after being damaged by warfare at the end of the Heian period, it was revived during the Muromachi period by a high-Ranking monk. In the Edo period it received the protection of the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Han]] of Bizen Province. Long revered for its Yogācāra Daigongen, following the Meiji [[Shinbutsu Bunri]] edict, a separate shrine – later known as {{ill|Yuka Shrine|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine|qq=}} – was established within the temple grounds.
After the post–World War II dissolution of State Shinto, although formally separated, the monastic staff continued to perform rituals in the main hall so that, in practice, the pre–Meiji state of affairs was restored. As a result, while Yuka Shrine retained its legal status as a separate religious corporation and maintained its own priests, it was in effect nearly dormant.
In 1997 (Heisei 9) on March 3, Yuka Shrine began independent religious activities by occupying part of the temple buildings. Since then Rendaiji and Yuka Shrine have been in conflict—with Rendaiji seeking the return of its buildings—though Yuka Shrine continues its independent operations.
A traditional custom called “Ryōmairi” (simultaneous pilgrimage to both shrines) developed in the Edo period in which pilgrims visited both the Yogācāra Daigongen of Yugasan and the {{ill|Kinpirō Daigongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpirō Daigongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金毘羅権現}} (now at [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kintōhira-gū]]) in Sanuki Province.
== Overview == It is widely venerated as a protective deity against misfortune. In 2007 (Heisei 19) a 7.59‑meter tall wooden statue of the immovable 【ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王】—the tallest of its kind in Japan—was completed.
The approach (sando) up Yuka-yama is lined by a traditional town (monzenchō) whose local specialty is the sweet rice cake known as {{ill|Ankoro Mochi|ja|あんころ餅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ankoro Mochi|qq=}}.
== Cultural Properties == ; Okayama Prefecture–designated Important Cultural Properties * The Nakazato Fujizuka (mound) – one of the fujizuka built by the Fuji-kō group (pilgrims to Mt. Fuji) from the Edo period to early Showa period. In Kurashiki it is one of the larger mounds, with a height of 12 meters from its southern base and a diameter of 30 meters. A stone monument dated to 1822 (Bunsei 5) indicates that its construction began in the Edo period. Among 36 stone structures, its dōsojin monument is unique in the district. ; Treasures * Ema (votive plaques) depicting the subjugation of the Yamata no Orochi (dating from Tenpō 5, July) * Ema commemorating a Fuji ascent (dating from Keien 7, June) * Ema depicting a pilgrimage to Ise (by Fujigoro and Murata Masatarō, dating from Keien 3, March)＜ref＞{{Cite book|和書|author=Ishikamin Library Local Materials Section|title=Ema of Nerima|publisher=Nerima City Board of Education|year=1978|page=175,178}}＜/ref＞ == Address == * 2855 Kojima Yuka, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture == Access == * By car: From the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Setchu Chūō Expressway]] at {{ill|Mizushima IC|ja|水島IC|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima IC|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水島インターチェンジ}}, take {{ill|Okayama Kojima Road No.21|ja|岡山児島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Kojima Road No.21|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道21号岡山児島線}} east toward Okayama, and at the “Gōnai” intersection turn east onto {{ill|Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|ja|玉野福田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Tamano Fukuda Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡山県道62号玉野福田線}}. Continue straight along the route – about 15 minutes by car. * By train: Approximately a 15‑minute taxi ride from [[:en:Kojima Station|Kojima Station]] on the [[:en:Seto-Ōhashi Line|Seto Ohashi Line]]. * By bus: A “Shimoden” bus (downward bus) runs from Kojima Station; however, there are only two round-trips per day (one in the morning, one in the evening) with a stop time of about 5 minutes. (Buses do not run on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or the New Year’s three days.) == Adjacent Sacred Sites == ; [[:en:Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|China 33 Kannon Pilgrimage]] : 5 {{ill|Hōkai-in|ja|法界院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-in|qq=}} – '''6 Yugasan Rendaiji''' – 7 {{ill|Entsū-ji|ja|円通寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Entsū-ji|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kurashiki High School Educational Research Group, Social Studies Division, History Section|title=New Edition: Historical Strolls of Okayama Prefecture|publisher=Yamakawa Shuppan|year=1991|pages=96–97}} * Local explanatory plaques == References ==
{{Reflist|group=2}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yuka Shrine Main|ja|由加神社本宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuka Shrine Main|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoshida Yoshi-dō|ja|柴田義董|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Yoshi-dō|qq=}} – credited with painting the 50‐faced barrier paintings at Rendaiji. == External Links == * {{Commonscat|Rendaiji}} * [https://yugasan.jp/ Yugasan Rendaiji Official Website] * [https://www.city.kurashiki.okayama.jp/bunkahogo/ Kurashiki City Cultural Properties Protection Division]
